title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
How Diabetes Affects Eye Health & Vision Over Time
Diabetes doesn’t just influence your sugar levels, but it can also slowly change the way you see the world. As per the ICMR-INDIAB reports, India now has about 101.3 million people with diabetes and 136 million with prediabetes, yet more than 57% remain undiagnosed. That silent gap is what drives complications like Diabetic Retinopathy (DR), which affects around 12.5% of people over 40, and Diabetic Macular Edema (DME), a vision-threatening condition seen in nearly 1 in 10 diabetics. With 1 in 3 diabetics expected to develop retinopathy over time, understanding how diabetes impacts vision is the first step towards protecting it for a lifetime.
How Diabetes and Eye Vision are Connected?
High blood sugar can slowly affect the way your eyes work. In the early stages, you may experience temporary blurry vision, especially when your treatment plan or medication changes. This happens because fluctuation in glucose levels alters the eye’s fluid balance. The genuine concern begins when blood sugar stays high in the long term. Over time, it weakens the tiny blood vessels at the back of the eye, causing leaks, swelling, or the growth of fragile new vessels that can bleed or raise eye pressure. Pregnancy for diabetics can speed up these changes, which is why regular eye checks are crucial for anyone with diabetes.
Common Eye Conditions Linked to Diabetes
Diabetes can gradually affect different parts of the eye, leading to conditions that may impact vision if not managed early.
When to Seek Help and How Doctors Check Your Eye Health?
If you ever notice sudden eyesight changes like quick flashes of light, a sudden shower of floaters, or a shadow that feels like a curtain sliding over your sight, it’s time to visit an ophthalmologist. These warning signs can indicate a retinal detachment, which requires emergency medical attention.
To find out what’s going on, doctors rely on a detailed, dilated eye exam, where special drops open your pupils so they can look closely at the back of your eye. They may also check your vision, measure eye pressure, and run additional tests based on your medical history.
For most people with diabetes, an annual eye exam is essential, although your care team may recommend more frequent visits depending on your diagnosis and overall health.
Treatment Options for Diabetes-Related Eye Problems
Managing diabetic eye disease usually involves tighter control of your blood sugar, blood pressure, and cholesterol, along with targeted treatments to protect your vision. Depending on the extent of the condition, doctors may use medicines, lasers, or surgery to prevent further damage.
Simple Ways to Protect Your Eyes
Here’s what you can do to protect your eyes right from the start to avoid vision loss:
Keep your blood sugar in a healthy range through medication, food choices, and daily activity.
Choose nutrient-rich foods, such as green leafy vegetables, berries, fish, and nuts, to support eye health.
Get a complete dilated eye exam every year (or as advised).
Quit smoking, as it speeds up eye damage and raises the risk of vision loss.
Wrapping Up
It’s not easy to lead a life without one of your sensory organs functioning properly. When you live with diabetes, you have to be extra careful about your eye care. The earlier you diagnose and treat any vision-related issue, the greater your chances of recovery. Being proactive through regular eye exams, maintaining stable blood sugar levels, and timely intervention can slow or prevent serious damage.
Key Takeaways
Almost 1 in 3 people with diabetes are affected by eye issues, leading to vision loss.
High blood sugar puts pressure on blood vessels, which can tear them in extreme cases.
Common eye diseases that diabetics suffer from: diabetic retinopathy, diabetic macular edema, glaucoma, and cataract.
Some symptoms of poor vision include quick flashes of light, floaters or dark spots, circles around lights, hazy vision, and difficulty differentiating colours.
Apart from monitoring glucose levels, treatments include anti-VEGF injections, laser therapy, vitrectomy, or cataract surgery.
For healthy eyesight, an annual eye check-up, a balanced meal, regular exercise, and quitting smoking are essential.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
What early eye symptoms should people with diabetes watch for?Look out for blurry vision, floaters, trouble seeing at night, or halos around lights. These small changes can signal early damage.
How often should someone with diabetes get an eye checkup?A yearly dilated eye exam is ideal. If you already have vision changes, your doctor may recommend more frequent visits.
What is the cost of an intravitreal injection?
In India, the price of this injection varies from ₹6,000 to ₹60,000 depending on the drugs used. Currently, CGHS and a few state schemes offer support for DR treatment.
Can diabetic eye problems be reversed?Early-stage issues often improve when blood sugar is controlled. Advanced damage may not be fully reversed, but timely treatment can slow progression.
Are diabetic eye treatments painful?Most treatments, including injections and laser procedures, are performed after the application of numbing drops. You may feel mild pressure, but it’s generally tolerable and quick.
Disclaimer
The content of this article is intended for informational purposes only and does not provide any professional medical/nutritional advice. Kindly consult your Dietician/Nutritionist/Doctor for anyassistance/advice. Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) disclaims any warranty, responsibility or liability for the accuracy of the above information and assumes no responsibility for errors or omissions.
full_pdf_text:
How Diabetes Affects Eye Health & Vision Over Time
Diabetes doesn’t just influence your sugar levels, but it can also slowly change the way you see the world. As per the ICMR-INDIAB reports, India now has about 101.3 million people with diabetes and 136 million with prediabetes, yet more than 57% remain undiagnosed.1 That silent gap is what drives complications like Diabetic Retinopathy (DR), which affects around 12.5% of people over 40, and Diabetic Macular Edema (DME), a vision-threatening condition seen in nearly 1 in 3diabetics. With 1 in 3 diabetics expected to develop retinopathy over time,1 understanding how diabetes impacts vision is the first step towards protecting it for a lifetime.
How Diabetes and Eye Vision are Connected? 2
High blood sugar can slowly affect the way your eyes work. In the early stages, you may experience temporary blurry vision, especially when your treatment plan or medication changes. This happens because fluctuation in glucose levels alters the eye’s fluid balance. The concern begins when blood sugar stays high in the long term. Over time, it weakens the tiny blood vessels at the back of the eye, causing leaks, swelling, or the growth of fragile new vessels that can bleed or raise eye pressure.
Common Eye Conditions Linked to Diabetes
Diabetes can gradually affect different parts of the eye, leading to conditions that may impact vision if not managed early.
Eye Condition What Happens 2 Common Symptoms 3 Diabetic Retinopathy
High sugar levels weaken and damage the tiny vessels in the retina. In early stages, they leak; in advanced stages, new fragile vessels grow and may bleed, raising the risk of severe vision loss.
Blurry or shifting vision, floaters or dark spots visible, trouble telling colours apart Diabetic Macular Edema
Swelling builds up in the macula, the area responsible for sharp, detailed vision. It often appears in people who already have retinopathy.
Hazy central vision, distorted images
Glaucoma Increased pressure inside the eye can damage the optic nerve. Diabetes almost doubles the risk, making early detection important.
Gradual side-vision loss, eye pain, headaches, circular glow around lights Cataract Excess sugar leads to faster clouding of the eye’s natural lens. People with diabetes tend to develop cataracts earlier, and the condition progresses quickly.
Cloudy vision, glare sensitivity, poor night vision
When to Seek Help and How Doctors Check Your Eye Health?2
If you ever notice sudden eyesight changes like quick flashes of light, a sudden shower of floaters, or a shadow that feels like a curtain sliding over your sight, it’s time to visit an ophthalmologist. These warning signs can indicate a diabetic retinopathy, which requires emergency medical attention.
To find out what’s going on, doctors rely on a detailed, dilated eye exam, where special drops open your pupils so they can look closely at the back of your eye. They may also check your vision, measure eye pressure, and run additional tests based on your medical history.
For most people with diabetes, an annual eye exam is essential, although your care team may recommend more frequent visits depending on your diagnosis and overall health.
Treatment Options for Diabetes-Related Eye Problems 2
Managing diabetic eye disease usually involves tighter control of your blood sugar, blood pressure, and cholesterol, along with targeted treatments to protect your vision. Depending on the extent of the condition, doctors may use medicines, lasers, or surgery to prevent further damage.
Treatment How It Helps Anti VEGF-Medicine Medicines are injected into the eye to stop abnormal blood vessel growth and stop fluid leak . Often used for diabetic macular edema and advanced retinopathy. Laser Therapy Focused light treats leaking vessels and slows disease progression. Includes focal/grid lasers for macular edema and PRP (panretinal photocoagulation) for widespread abnormal vessel growth in advanced retinopathy. Vitrectomy Surgery Removes blood-filled or scarred vitreous gel and helps repair severe retinal issues, especially when tissue pulls away. Cataract Surgery Replaces a cloudy natural lens with an artificial one, helping to restore clearer vision; this procedure is often needed earlier in people with diabetes.
Simple Ways to Protect Your Eyes
Here’s what you can do to protect your eyes right from the start to avoid vision loss:
1. Keep your blood sugar in a healthy range through medication, food choices, and daily
activity. 2. Choose nutrient-rich foods, such as green leafy vegetables, berries, fish, and nuts, to support
eye health. 3. Get a complete dilated eye exam every year (or as advised). 4. Quit smoking, as it speeds up eye damage and raises the risk of vision loss.
Wrapping Up
It’s not easy to lead a life without one of your sensory organs functioning properly. When you live with diabetes, you have to be extra careful about your eye care. The earlier you diagnose and treat any vision-related issue, the greater your chances of recovery. Being proactive through regular eye exams, maintaining stable blood sugar levels, and timely intervention can slow or prevent serious damage.
Key Takeaways
1. Almost 1 in 3 people with diabetes are affected by eye issues, leading to vision loss. 2. High blood sugar puts pressure on blood vessels, which can tear them in extreme cases.
3. Common eye diseases that diabetics suffer from: diabetic retinopathy, diabetic macular
edema, glaucoma, and cataract. 4. Some symptoms of poor vision include quick flashes of light, floaters or dark spots, circles
around lights, hazy vision, and difficulty differentiating colours. 5. Apart from monitoring glucose levels, treatments include anti-VEGF injections, laser therapy,
vitrectomy, or cataract surgery. 6. For healthy eyesight, an annual eye check-up, a balanced meal, regular exercise, and quitting
smoking are essential.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
1. What early eye symptoms should people with diabetes watch for?
Look out for blurry vision, floaters, trouble seeing at night, or halos around lights. These small changes can signal early damage. 2. How often should someone with diabetes get an eye checkup?
A yearly dilated eye exam is ideal. If you already have vision changes, your doctor may recommend more frequent visits. 3. What is the cost of an intravitreal injection?
In India, the price of this injection varies from ₹6,000 to ₹60,000 depending on the drugs used. Currently, CGHS and a few state schemes offer support for diabetic retinopathy (DR) treatment.1
4. Can diabetic eye problems be reversed?
Early-stage issues often improve when blood sugar is controlled. Advanced damage may not be fully reversed, but timely treatment can slow progression. 5. Are diabetic eye treatments painful?
Most treatments, including injections and laser procedures, are performed after the application of numbing drops. You may feel mild pressure, but it’s generally tolerable and quick.
Disclaimer
The content of this article is intended for informational purposes only and does not provide any professional medical/nutritional advice. Kindly consult your Dietician/Nutritionist/Doctor for any assistance/advice. Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) disclaims any warranty, responsibility or liability for the accuracy of the above information and assumes no responsibility for errors or omissions.
Source
1. https://www.newindianexpress.com/nation/2025/Apr/07/diabetes-linked-vision-loss-fear-
looms-large-experts-seek-ayushman-care
2. https://www.niddk.nih.gov/health-information/diabetes/overview/preventing-
problems/diabetic-eye-disease 3. https://netralayam.com/blog/how-diabetes-affects-your-eyes-signs-risks-and-ways-to-
protect-your-vision
